summary.intent: Bulk-import all 67 Hermes skills into Moltis as bundled assets, injecting origin metadata and updating the skill discovery code to handle arbitrarily nested directory structures.
summary.outcome: 69 skills across 18 categories successfully copied, origin metadata injected, discovery code made recursive, and all tests passing — committed and pushed as part of PR #797.

repo: Hermes skills live in a local clone and can be bulk-copied into Moltis bundled assets; `hermes-agent` and `native-mcp` are Hermes-internal and should be excluded.
The `mlops` skill category has 3+ levels of nesting (e.g. mlops/training/axolotl/SKILL.md), requiring recursive discovery rather than a flat or single-level scan.
Category-level DESCRIPTION.md files exist in Hermes and must be removed after copying — they are not valid skill files for Moltis.
Excluding `native-mcp` leaves the `mcp/` category directory empty; it must be explicitly removed after the copy.
All 69 SKILL.md frontmatter names passed Moltis validation without any transformation needed.

code.finding: Both FS and embedded skill discovery functions (list, find_skill_dir) were updated from flat/single-level to fully recursive to support arbitrarily nested category structures like mlops/training/axolotl.

workflow: Using `sed` to inject YAML frontmatter fields into multi-line files is fragile and can silently truncate file content; a Python script reading/writing the full file is the reliable approach.
When a bulk sed operation corrupts files, re-copying from the source and rerunning the correct script is faster than attempting in-place repair.
Verifying both a top-level and a deeply nested skill after metadata injection confirms the script handled all directory depths correctly.

friction: Initial `sed` command to inject `origin:` metadata corrupted SKILL.md files by truncating content, requiring a full re-copy from Hermes before retrying with Python.
Nested mlops sub-categories (3 levels deep) were not anticipated; the discovery code had to be refactored before the bulk copy could work correctly.
The manually created `arxiv` skill had to be deleted before copying so it would be replaced by the canonical Hermes version.
open_items: The `dogfood` skill (QA testing) was copied in — worth confirming it is genuinely useful in the Moltis context and not Hermes-specific in practice.
No automated check prevents Hermes-internal skills (`hermes-agent`, `native-mcp`) from being accidentally re-added in future sync operations; a blocklist or sync script would prevent recurrence.
Origin metadata references a specific Hermes commit SHA — a periodic resync process to keep bundled skills up to date with upstream Hermes changes is not yet defined.
